coredata.dc:title: Analysis of electrical parameters of p-i-n perovskites solar cells during passivation via N-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Firoz
Khan, Mohd Taukeer
Rehman, Shafiqur
Al-Sulaiman, Fahad
coredata.dc:description: Metal halide perovskite (PVSK) solar cells (PSCs) have arisen as emerging photovoltaics (PV) technology. Nevertheless, due to defects in the organic-inorganic metal-halide PVSK layer, the conversion efficiency (η) and the stability are hampered. Thus, the passivation of the perovskites layer is vital to obtain stable PSCs against temperature and humidity. Due to the introduction of the N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) interlayer at the PVSK/electron transport layer (ETL) interface, recombination in the PSCs is greatly suppressed. The NGQDs layer promotes charge transportation in the PVSK layer as well as at the PVSK/ETL interface, which enhances the performance of the PSCs. The performance of the PSCs is governed by the cell's electrical parameters. Thus, the electrical parameters are analyzed during PSC passivation. PVSK passivation with NGQDs consequences in the upsurge of the recombination lifetime (from 31.7 to 59.9 ns), improves the interfacial charge transfer and suppresses the trap density from 5.53×1016 cm−3 to 4.68×1016 cm−3, which are confirmed through photoluminescence, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and space charge limited (SCL) measurement, respectively. The conversion efficiency of the NGQDs passivated PSC is boosted to 17.10%, which is ∼13.8% higher than the control cell (η ∼15.03%, un-passivated PSC). Furthermore, the PV cell parameters are determined analytically, which revealed that both the ideality factor (n) and leakage current (J0 ) reduced to 1.2409 and 1.37 × 10−16 A/cm2, respectively (n = 1.6114 and J0 = 4.24 × 10−13 A/cm2 of the control cell). The present work synergically improves the charge transport and suppresses the non-radiative recombination losses in PSCs via defect passivation through NGQDs which are essential for further advancement of the device performance and operational stability.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal halide perovskite (PVSK) solar cells (PSCs) have arisen as emerging photovoltaics (PV) technology. Nevertheless, due to defects in the organic-inorganic metal-halide PVSK layer, the conversion efficiency (η) and the stability are hampered. Thus, the passivation of the perovskites layer is vital to obtain stable PSCs against temperature and humidity. Due to the introduction of the N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) interlayer at the PVSK/electron transport layer (ETL) interface, recombination in the PSCs is greatly suppressed. The NGQDs layer promotes charge transportation in the PVSK layer as well as at the PVSK/ETL interface, which enhances the performance of the PSCs. The performance of the PSCs is governed by the cell's electrical parameters.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decade, metal halide perovskite (PVSK) solar cells (PSCs) have emerged as the fastest mounting photovoltaics (PVs) technology. These cells have achieved a power conversion efficiency (PCE; η) of more than 25.5%, which is higher than the commercially available thin-film PV technologies and competing with c-Si solar cells [1]. Despite excellent PCE, several issues including electrical instability, non-radiative recombination, long-term operational stability, anomalous J-V hysteresis, etc. remain the bottleneck for the industrial commercialization of PSCs. The primary reason for such a limiting behavior is the presence of a high density of defects (1015 – 1020 cm−3) in the PVSK layer [2] due to very low defect formation energy (< 0.5 eV) [3, 4] and low activation energy for ionic migration [5].

However, the PVSK materials are defect-tolerant because the majority of defect states reside either in the valence or conduction band. The defects at the grain boundaries or at the PVSK surface or at PVSK/charge transfer layer (CTL) interface create trap states in the bandgap of the PVSKs. During the operation of the PSCs, the charge carriers are trapped in these trapping states, thus, the transportation of the charge carriers is hampered. Consequently, it limits the cell performance. The presence of traps causes Shockley-Read-Hall (SRH) non-radiative recombination [6], J-V hysteresis [7], charge carrier scattering [8], shortening of the electron-hole diffusion length, and drop-in device open-circuit voltage (Voc ) [9]. The existence of defect sites at the PVSK surface and grain boundaries causes the deterioration of the cell performance because these sites allow the infiltration of ambient oxygen and moisture into the PVSK layer. Hence, in order to commercialize the PSCs technology and achieve the PCE close to Shockley–Queisser limit (SQL), the defect sites should be diminished.

The defects at the grain boundaries are passivated by mixing with an additive substance in the PVSK precursor solutions or antisolvents. On the other hand, the surface defects are passivated by inserting an interlayer at PVSK/CTL interfaces. The negatively charged vacancies created due to halide ion migration/halide vacancies are passivated via metal cations (Na+, K +, Cd2+) [[10–12]. Here, the halide functional group (NaF) or halide ions (Cl−, F −) have been employed to passivate the positively charged defects [13, 14]. The usage of additive such as 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane [15], 2,3,5,6-Tetrafluoro-7,7,8,8-tetracyanoquinodimethane (F4TCNQ) [16], hydrophilic fullerene [17], phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) [18], alkyl ammonium salt [19], pentaerythritol tetrakis(3-mercaptopropionate) [20], fluorinated ionic liquid [21, 22], phenylethylammonium iodine and Pb(SCN)2 [23], Pentafluorophenethylammonium (F5PEA+) [24] in PVSK precursor decelerate the ionic migration, passivate charged defects, reduces hysteresis, improves the crystallinity and slow down the cell degradation. The mixed additives synergistically improve perovskite crystallinity, passivate grain boundaries, and improve carrier mobility and lifetime [25–27]. The passivation of the perovskites layer with Lewis acids/bases also reduces the defects at the grain boundaries [25, 28-32]. The organic molecules containing Lewis's acid/base functional groups such as 2-mercaptopyridine, phenylalkylamine, pyridine, thiophene, and carbonyl have been employed to passivate the Pb2+ defects, which are considered the main recombination paths [33–37].

Interfacial passivation suppresses the recombination centers present at PVSK/CTL interface as well as allows the optimization of energy levels of the PVSK photoactive layer and CTL. This consequently reduces the recombination, enhances the charge collection efficiency, and the stability. The introduction of a thin heterocyclic halides layer of imidazolium iodide [38] and thiazolium iodide [39] at the PVSK/CTL interface improves the photovoltaic parameters by passivating the iodide vacancies, reducing the shallow as well as deep traps, and suppressing non-radiative recombination. The passivation of mixed cations and anions-based PSCs with 4‑tert-butylpyridine (4-tBP) show better performance in terms of improved η and Voc but at the same time negatively affect the cell stability [40]. Though the PSC passivated with phenethylammonium iodide and butylammonium iodide has shown a significant improvement in Voc through the suppression of interface defects [41–45].

Recently, intensive interest has grown in the dimensional engineering of perovskite films by introducing large organic cations, e.g. alkylammonium halide into the A-site of the ABX3 perovskite which transforms a 3D perovskite crystal into a 2D or quasi-2D layered structure [41, 46-50]. The usage of organic cations also passivates the negative charge defects and improves the operational stability of the device. The post-treatment of triple cation perovskites with tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate led to the formation of a 2D/3D heterostructure. This also passivates the grain boundaries of the PVSK layers [51]. The direct passivation of the perovskite layer with 4-fluorophen ethylammonium iodide (F-PEAI) suppresses the charge trapping process by impeding the formation of the iodine vacancies [52]. Dual passivation by N, N’-bis-(7,7,8,8,9,9,10,10,11,11,12,12,12-tridecafluoro dodecan-5-yl)-perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic diimide in chlorobenzene as the antisolvent treatment and then a 2D perovskites layer was in-situ synthesized by introducing a hydrophobic layer of 2-(2-Fluorophenyl)ethylamine iodide layer on top of the annealed PVSK layer [53]. In another work, the Voc and fill factor (FF) was found to be improved via the insertion of the hexylammonium iodide passivation layer on top of PVSK [54].

Despite such advantages, 2D perovskites exhibit a wider bandgap and large-sized organic cations used in the 2D-perovskites synthesis often form a charge transport barrier at the interface [55–57]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have better charge transport properties compared to other passivating materials and are cost-effective. The passivation of the PVSK layer with an amphiphilic interlayer of nitrogen functionalized GQDs (NGQDs) improves the PVSK/CTL interface and also controls the passivation layer thickness due to its small size, which is challenging in organic cations having long-chain [58]. The lone pair electrons in nitrogen can interact with cations and passivate the positive charge defects. Combining all these properties make GQDs a potential material for retarding electron recombination in PSCs. Previously graphene oxide (GO) [59] and N-doped graphene [60] has been in-situ incorporated in the precursor solution and obtained an improvement in the device performance. The multilayer graphene has also been used at PVSK/ZnO interface to protect the device from degradation [61]. Pang et al. [62] used GQD decorated SnO2 electron transport layer (ETL) to improve the performance of the PSCs. The photogenerated current density (Jph) was increased due to photon absorption enhancement, which improve the short circuit current density (Jsc) by 1.8 mA/cm2. Zhang et al. [63] have added GQDs in the PVSK precursor to obtain a pinhole-free PVSK layer. They achieved an efficiency improvement of 11%. Fang et al. [64] passivated the grain boundaries of PVSK using GQDs. Gan et al. [65] used NGQD additives to passivate the PVSK grain boundaries. They observed that the N active sites contribute to the crystallization of the PVSK layer and passivate the defects at grain boundaries via Lewis acid/base interaction. Owing to the incorporation of NGQDs, Jph was enhanced.The resistive and recombination losses were reduced. The resistive losses are represented by series (Rs) and shunt (Rsh) resistances, whereas diode ideality factor (n) and reverse saturation current density (J0) signify the recombination losses. These photovoltaic (PV) cell parameters (Jph, Rsh, Rs, n, and J0) govern the performance parameters (Jsc, Voc, FF, and η) of the PSCs. Thus, to understand the loss mechanism in the PSC, investigation of the PV cell parameters is vital.

The present investigation, report the passivation of inverted (p-i-n) PSCs using NGQDs interlayer at PVSK/ETL interface. To the best of authors understanding, NGQDs have not been used as a passivation layer on top of perovskites films. The device performance is found to be improved compared to the control cell (PSC without passivation). Furthermore, PV cell parameters are examined for loss analysis. It is found that the recombination loss was significantly abridged along with a slight reduction in the resistive loss via insertion of the NGQDs layer at the PVSK/ETL interface. The present work offers an alternative and cost-effective material for the surface passivation of PSCs.

Nickel nitrate hexahydrate, copper nitrate trihydrate, zinc acetate dihydrate, sodium hydroxide, perovskites grade lead iodide, methylammonium iodide (MAI, CH3NH3I), and PCBM were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The anhydrous solvents chlorobenzene, N,N-dimethylformamide, (DMF), and isopropanol were also purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Graphene oxide (GO) powder was synthesized as reported elsewhere [66].

Graphene oxide solution was prepared by dissolving synthesized GO in deionized (DI) water with a concentration of 4.0 mg/ml and ultrasonicated for 4 h [66]. In the GO solution, polyethyleneimine solution (PEI, Mw = 1800, 0.5 g/mL in DI water) was added to achieve PEI/GO volume ratio of 20, followed by stirring at 90 °C for 4 h to functionalize the GO with nitrogen [67]. The N-functionalized GO was heated at 300 °C for 1 h in a horizontal furnace under ambient Air followed by freeze-drying. The obtained product was dispersed in chlorobenzene by sonication. Finally, the dispersed solution was filtered using a 0.1 µm PTFE syringe filter to obtain a light-yellow solution of N-functionalized graphene quantum dots with ultra-high quantum yield [68].

The Zn-precursor solution was prepared by stirring zinc acetate dihydrate (26.8 mmol) in 250 mL of methanol. When zinc acetate dihydrate completely dissolved, KOH/methanol mixture solution was added dropwise into the Zn-precursor solution and continuously stirred for 4 h at 65 °C. When the reaction was accomplished, the precipitated ZnO nanoparticles (NPs) were washed several times with methanol and dispersed in n-butanol [69].

Copper-doped nickel oxide NPs were synthesized via the thermal decomposition method. First, 0.95 mmoles of nickel (II) nitrate hexahydrate [Ni(NO3)2•6H2O] and 0.05 mmole of cupric nitrate trihydrate [Cu(NO3)2•3 H2O], both procured from Sigma-Aldrich, were dissolved in 10 mL methanol and stirred for 2 h at 65 °C. Next, NaOH solution was added dropwise into it until the pH value of the solution became more than 10, followed by further stirring until the solution color turned green. The precipitate of the solution was washed several times in methanol by centrifuge and dried in an oven at 70 °C for 2 days. The dried product was further heated at 400 °C in the air for 1 h to achieve the NiO:Cu NPs and finally dispersed in n-butanol [70].

ITO coated glass substrates were cleaned by successive ultrasonication in a soap solution, DI water, ethanol, and acetone. The cleaned substrates were treated in UV ozone for 20 min. On top of the cleaned ITO, three layers of synthesized NiO:Cu (see supporting information) as the hole transport layer (HTL) was spin-casted at 4000 rpm for 30 s. On top of HTL, the PVSK layer was coated via two-step method. So, PbI2 and CH3NH3I solutions were prepared in anhydrous DMF (462 mg/ml) and anhydrous isopropanol (6 mg/mL), respectively. As a first step, the PbI2 solution was spin-casted on top of fabricated NiO:Cu film at 3000 rpm for 20 s followed by thermal annealing for 5 min at 100 °C. After colling the PbI2 films cooled to room temperature, MAI films were spin-casted at 4000 rpm for 30 s by dispensing 200 μL of CH3NH3I solution. During the last 10 s of the CH3NH3I spinning process, NGQDs solution (40 – 150 μL) was poured on spinning substrates as a passivation layer followed by thermal annealing at 100 °C for 10 min to achieve the MAPbI3 perovskite films. The ETL was deposited on top of MAPbI3 film by spin-casting of PCBM solution (20 mg/mL in chlorobenzene) at 1000 rpm for 60 s. Finally, back contact was applied on top of the PCBM layer via E-beam deposition of Ag metal of ˜ 150 nm. The PSC with NGQD contents of 40, 80, and 150 μL are referred to as PVSK-NGQD40, PVSK-NGQD80, and PVSK-NGQD150, respectively. Moreover, the control and champion (PVSK-NGQD80) PSCs are represented by PVSK and PVSK-NGQD, respectively. The electron-only device was fabricated on FTO coated glasses substrates and cleaned as discussed above. On top of cleaned FTO substrates, an undoped ZnO hole blocking layer was prepared using the atomic layer deposition (ALD) followed by spin-casting of synthesized ZnO nanoparticles (see supporting information). The MAPbI3, PCBM, and NGQD layers were deposited as explained above. Finally, back contact of 1.5 µm thickness was applied by e-beam evaporation of Al metal.

Hitachi field emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM), HF-3300, and Hitachi field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), S-4800 were, respectively, employed to record the high-resolution images of the NGQDs and surface morphology of perovskite films. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of PVSK films were recorded via a Panalytical HR-XRD system having Cu/Kα radiation of λ = 1.5406 Å. The elemental state of perovskite films was measured using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in an ultrahigh vacuum setup equipped with a monochromatic Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6 eV) and a high-resolution analyzer. The binding energies were calibrated based on the graphite C1s peak. The PVSK films were excited with a wavelength of 405 nm to measure the photoluminescence (PL) steady-state emissions and time-resolved PL (TRPL) decay spectra through Hamamatsu, C10627 fluorescence lifetime measurement system. The steady-state PL and TRPL spectra were recorded for the PVSK and PVSK/NGQDs samples prepared on glass substrates as discussed above. The solar cell's performance parameters were evaluated from current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics measured in the forward and reverse directions through Keithley, 2400 source meter, and devices were illuminated through New-port, 91,192 solar simulators. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) of fabricated devices was determined via the IPCEX7 series IPCE measurement system made by PV Measurements, Inc., USA. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra were obtained using VersaSTAT3 Potentiostate (M/s. Princeton Applied Research, USA) from 0.5 Hz to 1 MHz and ac perturbation of 10 mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of capacitance from constant phase element
ce:section.ce:para:
The capacitance C1 corresponds to geometrical capacitance and bulk dielectric relaxation of the PVSK layer and is determined from the constant phase element (CPE) in the low-frequency region (CPE1) through the relation (6) [71]:

The particle size distribution of the synthesized N-doped GQDs was recorded by HRTEM images shown in Fig 1 a. The HRTEM images reveal the quasi-spherical nanoparticles of a uniform size of 5 nm. Raman spectra of N-doped GQDs shown in Fig 1b exhibits D and G bands positioned at 1339 and 1562 cm−1, respectively, which are corresponding to graphene [73, 74]. However, the peaks obtained for GO are located at 1347 and 1590 cm−1, indicating that these peaks are blue-shifted due to size reduction [68, 72]. The d-band originates from the disorders in sp2 hybridized carbon and G-band corresponds to the in-phase vibration of graphite lattice. The intensity of the disordered band (D-band) is quite low compared to the ordered G-band attributed highly crystalline nature of synthesized GQDs [73], which have better electrical properties [74]. The peak at 1441 cm−1 corresponds to N-doped GQDs, further confirming the Nitrogen doping in graphene quantum dots [74]. To further demonstrate the success of N-doping in GQD, FTIR measurement was conducted. The FTIR spectra of NGQDs along with GO are shown in Fig 1c. The Band in the range of 2934 – 2881 cm−1 and at 1103 cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching vibrations, whereas the C = C vibration band appears at 1645 cm−1 and 1440 cm−1. The N-loading in GQDs was confirmed by the band observed at 1345 cm−1 corresponds to CN stretching [75].

The XRD spectra with and without passivated methylammonium lead iodide (MAPbI3) perovskites thin films are shown in Fig 2 a. The respective diffraction peaks observed at 2θ = 14.05°, 19.99°, 23.45°, 24.55°, 28.45°, 31.85°, 40.55° and 43.55° correspond to (110), (200), (211), (202), (220), (310), (224), and (314) planes of tetragonal phase of MAPbI3 [76–78]. Passivation treatment of the PVSK layer resulted in all peaks slightly (0.05°- 0.1°) shifting to a higher angle in comparison to un-passivated PVSK layer (represented as PVSK). The shift towards higher diffraction angles implies a decrease in the lattice plane distance attributed to a more crystalline structure [79]. Expectedly, no significant changes in the main diffraction peaks correspond to (110) and (220) plane of MAPbI3 perovskites films with or without passivation were noticed. This indicates that the passivation treatment does not influence the major crystal structure of perovskite.

Fig 2b schematically illustrates the interactions between N-doped GQDs and PVSK. The lone pair electron of N interacts with uncoordinated Pb2+ and passivating uncoordinated Pb2+defect states. The FESEM images of un-passivated and passivated PVSK layers are shown in Fig 2c and 2d, respectively. The FESEM images of the PVSK layer show crystalline flakes of uniform grain size. There are some cracks in the un-passivated PVSK layer, which are visible (white circles) between grains. These cracks act as non-radiative recombination centers and cause deterioration of device performance. In passivated PVSK layer, the gaps are passivated by NGQD (red circle) via interaction between the lone pair of N and Pb2+ as shown in Fig 2b. To further investigate the impact of NGQDs passivation on the PVSK layer, the XPS measurements were carried out. Fig 3 displays the high-resolution XPS spectra of the Pb 4f peak. In the PVSK layer, two characteristic peaks corresponding to Pb 4f5/2 and Pb 4f7/2 are obtained at binding energy (BE) of 143.6 and 138.7 eV, respectively. After insertion of NGQDs in the PVSK layer, these peaks are shifted towards low BE of 143.4 and 138.5 eV, respectively. This revealed that electron haze density on Pb is augmented due to lone pair electron presence on N moiety in the NGQDs. A similar observation was made in polyacrylonitrile passivated CsPbI3 [80].

The steady-state PL spectra of the NGQD passivated PVSK layer exhibits a blue shift of 3 nm and enhancement in PL intensity compared to un-passivated PVSK layer (Fig. 4a) . Due to the presence of the PL peak position of pristine NGQDs at ∼500 nm, the peak of PVSK-NGQD was blue-shifted [63, 68]. An enhancement in the PL intensity of the passivated PVSK layer confirms the suppression of nonradiative recombination channels due to the passivation of surface defects through NGQD. The blue shift in PL spectra also confirms an appropriate passivation with trap states [33]. The TRPL spectra were measured to investigate the impact of NGQD passivation on nonradiative recombination centers in the PVSK layer. It can be seen from Fig 4b that PL decay in NGQDs passivated PVSK is slower than in the un-passivated PVSK layer. The PL decay spectra were fitted with a two-component exponential decay model as given in eq. (8) [70].

The obtained values of τavg of the un-passivated PVSK and PVSK-NGQD are 31.7 ns and 59.9 ns, which indicates that the value of τavg of the PVSK-NGQD layer is more than twice the τavg of un-passivated PVSK. Thus, the obtained results confirm the mitigation of non-radiative recombination centers in the PVSK-NGQD layer [53].

The PSCs in this study were fabricated in planer inverted p-i-n structured viz. indium tin oxide (ITO)/NiO:Cu/CH3NH3PbI3/PCBM/Ag and ITO/NiO:Cu/CH3NH3PbI3/NGQDs/PCBM/Ag, as shown in Fig 5 a. During the PVSK layer coating, the NGQD solution (40, 80, and 150 μL) was poured to passivated the perovskite layer as well as charge transfer layer creation. It was observed that the performance of the PSC was improved via insertion NGQD of 40 μL. The best performance was obtained for 80 μL of NGQD solution. However, the PSC deteriorated for the higher volume of NGQD solution (150 μL) (Fig S1). The obtained values of performance parameters are listed in Table 2 and Table S1. Six devices of each configuration were measured and the corresponding J-V statistics for all PV parameters are illustrated in Fig S2. The measured J-V characteristics under illumination and the corresponding performance parameters for perovskites solar cells with (champion) and without passivation are illustrated in Fig 5b. The control device yielded a decent η of 15.03% with Jsc of 20.42 mA/cm2, Voc of 1.017 V, and FF of 0.724. The results revealed that the improvement in the η was ˜ 13.8% after insertion of the NGQD passivating layer in between the PVSK and ETL. Thus, the achieved efficiency of the champion PSC was 17.10%. The improved device performance was mainly observed due to an increase of Jsc (∼6.0% relative to the control cell) to 21.64 mA/cm2 and FF (from 72.4% to 75.9%), whereas a slight increment in Voc of 0.024 V was also observed. Thus, it is confirmed that the insertion of the NGQDs doesn't impact the electronic structure of the PSCs. The improved Jsc primarily occurs due to improved charge transfer between PVSK/ETL interfaces, confirmed via PL quenching shown in Fig S3. However, a slight enhancement in the Voc is attributed to the passivation of nonradiative recombination centers at the PVSK/ETL interface as confirmed through the enhanced PL intensity and longer carrier lifetime of passivated perovskites compared to the non-passivated PVSK layer (Fig 4a and b).

To investigate the impact of NGQDs passivation on the hysteresis behavior of the best PSCs, the J-V characteristics were recorded in both forward (f) and reverse directions as shown in Fig S4 and the corresponding performance parameters are listed in Table S2. It was noted that the hysteresis index (HI) of the passivated device (HI = 1.58%) was abridged as compared to the control device (HI = 2.33%). Furthermore, the stability of the PSCs was investigated at 25 °C under relative humidity (RH) of 28 ± 2%. The efficiency reduction of PSC was greatly abridged via insertion of the NGQDs (Fig S5). The initial efficiency was reduced to 75.9 and 86.1% for PVSK and PVSK-NGQD cells, respectively. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra and integrated current densities (Jsc ) obtained by integrating the EQE spectra over the solar spectrum are illustrated in Fig 5c. The integrated current densities obtained from the EQE spectra for passivated and un-passivated devices were 21.58 mA/cm2 and 20.39 mA/cm2, respectively, which are consistent with the results obtained from the J–V measurement.

To better understand the improved performance of NGQD passivated device, we evaluated the defect density and electrical parameters from the dark J-V characteristics of the un-passivated and passivated PVSK layer using an electron-only device configuration viz. fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO)/TiO2/CH3NH3PbI3/PCBM/Al and FTO/TiO2/CH3NH3PbI3/NGQDs/PCBM/Al, respectively, as shown in Fig 6 . The measured dark J-V characteristics exhibit three regions: (i) ohmic (ii) trap filling and (iii) trap filled. The electrical conductivity was estimated from the region (i) through the relation (10),
(10)
σ =
J L
/

where L is the thickness of the perovskites layer. The conductivity of the un-passivated PVSK layer was calculated to be 9.56×10−9 Scm−1 which increased to 3.63×10−8 Scm−1 when passivated with NGQDs. With further increase of applied bias, the injected carrier started filling the traps and the current density rapidly increased (region ii). When all trap states were occupied by the injected charge carriers, the current growth rate [d(J)/d(V)] decreased. Total trap density Ht was evaluated from the crossover voltage VC, as shown in Fig 6 using eq. (11) [81].

It was noted from Fig 6 that the crossover voltage for passivated perovskites device was decreased to 2.34 V in comparison to 2.55 V for the un-passivated device. The determined values of the corresponding trap densities were 6.08×1016 cm−3 and 4.90×1016 cm−3, respectively. A decrease in trap density confirms the passivation of defects by NGQD. Region (ii) was found to be well fitted (solid black line) with exponentially distributed trap limited space charge limited conduction (SCLC) eq. (12) [82, 83].

The value of nc is found to be higher in passivated devices further confirming the defect passivation through NGQD.

EIS is a powerful technique to analyze the interface of PSCs [85]. To analyze the impact of NGQDs passivation on interfacial charge transfer and recombination process in perovskites solar cells, the EIS spectra was measured as shown in Fig 7 . EIS spectra showed an arc at high frequency and a linear region at low frequency. The equivalent circuit shown in the inset of Fig 7 was used to fit the experimental EIS spectra, where R1 is the series resistance due to contact, constant phase element (CPE); CPE1 and CPE2 are the capacitance are high and low-frequency capacitances, respectively. The capacitance C1 corresponds to the geometrical capacitance of the PVSK layer and was found to be 1.04×10−8 F for both passivated and un-passivated devices (using eq. (6)). The charge transport resistance R2 in bulk perovskites was noted to drop to 1742 Ω after NGQDs passivation compared to 2661 Ω for the control device. The capacitance C2 corresponding to low-frequency mechanism including ionic transport, interfacial charge accumulation, and the transport was slightly decreased from 2.98×10−8 to 1.94×10−8 F (using eq. (7)). The decrease in interfacial capacitance indicates the decrease of interfacial charge accumulation due to defect passivation. The interfacial charge transfer resistance R3 was noted to decrease by around one order from 1.39×106 Ω to 6.071×105 Ω, indicating passivation improves the charge carrier dynamics between perovskites and the electron transport layer.

To further elucidate the impact of NGQD passivation on charge recombination, the PV cell parameters, that reveal the mechanism of recombination losses that occurring in solar cells, were analytically calculated as reported previously [68, 69, 86], by using the values of msc, moc, Vm, and Jm. The values of msc, moc, Jm , and Vm (listed in Table S3) are used in the eq. (1) – (5) of the supporting information. The obtained values of the PV cell parameters are listed in Table S4. The value of Jph was found to be higher for the passivated device compared to the control device. The shunt resistor represents the current loss due to the recombination of charge carriers and leakage current. Rsh was found to be increased from 1023.77 Ωcm2 for un-passivated PSC to 1076.81 Ωcm2 for the passivated cell, indicating suppression of recombination losses when passivated with the NGQDs. However, the value of Rs was reduced from 4.675 to 4.025 Ωcm2 via insertion of the NGQDs layer, which again confirms that the insertion of the NGQD layer in the PSC slightly improved the charge transportation. Thus, the overall combined resistance of the PSC was reduced. The n value for un-passivated PSC was calculated to be 1.611, which is significantly reduced to 1.241 for NGQD passivated PSC. This further affirmed the reduction of recombination defects in the PVSK layer due to NGQD passivation.

The reverse saturation current density is simply represented by the leakage current. It is the current flows in the opposite direction due to asymmetry in the energy levels of different layers of perovskites solar cells required for charge separation. This reduced the net photocurrent, therefore for a good device, J
0 should be minimum. The ideality factor and leakage current provide information about interfacial charge recombination at PVSK/CTL. It can be noted from Table S4 that the passivated device has a lower leakage current of 4.244×10−13 A/cm2 compared to un-passivated device with J0 of 1.368×10−16 A/cm2. The massive reduction in the values of n and J0 is attributed to substantial reduction in carrier recombination of the passivated device.

Furthermore, the changes in the performance and PV cell parameters were determined for the gain and loss analysis. Fig 8 a illustrates the changes in performance parameters. It can be seen that the Jsc was improved by 1.57% and 5.98% for the NGQD contents of 40 and 80 μL, respectively. However, Jsc was reduced by 4.95% for NGQD content of 150 μL. The Voc value was improved by 2.42% for NGQD content of 80 μL, whereas it was reduced by 1.32% for 80 μL. Moreover, the FF was greatly improved by 4.83% for the PVSK-NGQD80, which was worsened for the PVSK-NGQD80. The overall efficiency improvement of PVSK-NQGD80 was 13.8%. However, the η was reduced by −9.6% for the higher contents of NGQDs. This performance degradation of the performance of the PSC may be attributed to the dissolving of the PVSK layer, which deteriorates the layer quality.

For further loss analysis, the changes in the PV cell parameters are determined (Fig 8b and c). The value of shunt resistance of PVSK-NGQD150 was hugely reduced (34.88%). The n and J0 were reduced by ∼23% and ∼100%, respectively for the champion PSC, which is an indicative of good passivation of the device. However, n and J0 were increased for PVSK-NGQD150, which revealed that recombination was enhanced due to the deterioration of the PVSK layer.

In summary, this study, successfully demonstrated that NGQD passivation to suppresses the non-radiative recombination and improves the charge transport in planer p-i-n PSCs. As a result, NGQD passivated device performance was improved to 17.10% compared to 15.03% of the control device, attributed to an increase of Jsc to 21.64 mA/cm2, reduction of n and J0 values to 1.241 and 1.368×10−16 A/cm2, respectively compared to Jsc = 20.42 mA/cm2, n = 1.611, and J0 = 4.244 × 10−13 A/cm2 of the control device. NGQD passivation led to a decrease of trap density to 4.68×1016 cm−3 and an increase of charge carrier lifetime to 59.9 ns compared to 5.53×1016 cm−3 and 31.7 ns, respectively, for the controlled device. Further, NGQD passivation resulted in the reduction of interfacial charge transfer resistance around one order (from 1.39×106 to 6.071×105 Ω) and an increase in charge carrier mobility (from 5.10×10−4 to 8.30×10−4 cm2/Vs), reveals the passivation improve the charge carrier dynamics between perovskites and electron transport layer. This work indicates that NGQD passivation has a significant potential for synergically passivated defects and improved charge transfer at perovskites/ETL in p-i-n PSCs. The performance of the PSCs can be further improved via bulk passivation using NGQDs.
